ruleClauses: You may have a favorable-outcome annulment pathway. The next step is to confirm the disposition date and whether the record was automatically annulled or still needs a petition.
Not Guilty, Dismissed, or Not Prosecuted
Wilma should first ask whether the case ended without a conviction.

For offenses disposed of before January 1, 2019, a person whose arrest resulted in a finding of not guilty, dismissal, or non-prosecution may petition for annulment at any time. For offenses disposed of on or after January 1, 2019, RSA 651:5 says qualifying not-guilty, dismissed, or not-prosecuted records shall be annulled 30 days after the finding if no appeal is taken, or after final appeal determination if an appeal is taken. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/new-hampshire/title-lxii/chapter-651/section-651-5/")
Did the case end in a conviction, or was it dismissed, not prosecuted, or resolved with a not-guilty finding?

If a conviction was later vacated by a court, RSA 651:5 allows annulment of the arrest record, court record, or both. For qualifying post-2019 vacated convictions, the statute says the arrest and court record shall be annulled. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/new-hampshire/title-lxii/chapter-651/section-651-5/")
Was the conviction later vacated, reversed, or set aside by the court?
Conviction Annulment Under RSA 651:5
For convictions, the person generally must:
1 Complete all terms and conditions of the sentence;
2 Have no new disqualifying conviction during the waiting period;
3 Satisfy the waiting period for the offense level;
4 Have no pending charge that would block or extend eligibility;

5 Avoid statutory bars, including violent crimes, felony obstruction-of-justice crimes, and extended-term sentences. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/new-hampshire/title-lxii/chapter-651/section-651-5/")
For a conviction, New Hampshire usually starts counting after the sentence is fully complete, not just from the conviction date.
Marijuana Possession Annulment

New Hampshire has a special marijuana possession annulment statute, RSA 651:5-b. A person arrested or convicted before September 16, 2017 for knowingly or purposely possessing, obtaining, purchasing, or transporting 3/4 of an ounce of marijuana or less may petition the court where the arrest or conviction occurred at any time. The prosecutor has 10 days to object; if the prosecutor objects, they must prove beyond a reasonable doubt that the marijuana amount exceeded 3/4 ounce. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/new-hampshire/2019/title-lxii/chapter-651/section-651-5-b/")
Was this for possession of marijuana, and was the amount 3/4 ounce or less before September 16, 2017?

Important update: a 2025 bill, HB 196, would have expanded cannabis annulment, but it did not become law; it was killed in the Senate. Wilma should not apply that proposed expansion as current law. ("https://www.mpp.org/states/new-hampshire/?utm_source=chatgpt.com")

DWI is a special category. New Hampshires main statute carves out RSA 265-A:21 from the ordinary waiting-period structure. 603 Legal Aid explains that convictions for driving, attempting to drive, or operating a vehicle, OHRV, or boat while under the influence generally require a 10-year waiting period after conviction before asking for annulment. ("https://www.603legalaid.org/how-to-remove-your-criminal-record")
Wilma should treat DWI as an escalation flag:
DWI has special annulment rules in New Hampshire. We need to verify the exact statute, conviction date, sentence completion, and whether any other convictions affect timing.
Federal, Military, or Out-of-State Records

New Hampshire courts can only annul New Hampshire records. 603 Legal Aid explains that New Hampshire courts cannot remove federal or military convictions, and out-of-state records require relief in the state where they occurred. ("https://www.603legalaid.org/how-to-remove-your-criminal-record")
A New Hampshire annulment petition can address New Hampshire arrests, charges, convictions, and sentences. It does not directly annul a federal, military, Tribal, or out-of-state conviction.
If no conviction: You may have a favorable-outcome annulment pathway.

sourceSections.title: Core New Hampshire Rule
sourceSections.text:
In New Hampshire, annulment can apply to records of:

Record Type Possible Annulment?
Arrest Yes
Court record Yes
Conviction Yes, if eligible
Sentence Yes, if eligible
Dismissed / not prosecuted case Yes
Not guilty case Yes
Vacated conviction Yes
Marijuana possession record Special pathway
Federal conviction Not through NH court
Out-of-state conviction Not removed by NH court
Wilma should explain:

In New Hampshire, record clearing is usually called annulment. If granted, the record is sealed from ordinary public access and you are generally treated as if you had not been arrested, convicted, or sentenced, but law enforcement, courts, and some agencies may still access it in limited situations.

The effect is strong, but not absolute. RSA 651:5 says court records relating to an annulled arrest, conviction, or sentence are sealed and available only to the person, their attorney, courts for sentencing, law enforcement for legitimate law-enforcement purposes, or other limited statutory uses. (

"https://law.justia.com/codes/new-hampshire/title-lxii/chapter-651/section-651-5/")

sourceSections.title: Main New Hampshire Pathways
sourceSections.text:
Path A - Not Guilty, Dismissed, or Not Prosecuted
Wilma should first ask whether the case ended without a conviction.
For offenses disposed of before January 1, 2019, a person whose arrest resulted in a finding of not guilty, dismissal, or non-prosecution may petition for annulment at any time. For offenses disposed of on or after January 1, 2019, RSA 651:5 says qualifying not-guilty, dismissed, or not-prosecuted records shall be annulled 30 days after the finding if no appeal is taken, or after final appeal determination if an appeal is taken. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/new-hampshire/title-lxii/chapter-651/section-651-5/")
Wilma intake question:

Did the case end in a conviction, or was it dismissed, not prosecuted, or resolved with a not-guilty finding?

If no conviction:

You may have a favorable-outcome annulment pathway. The next step is to confirm the disposition date and whether the record was automatically annulled or still needs a petition.

Path B - Vacated Conviction
If a conviction was later vacated by a court, RSA 651:5 allows annulment of the arrest record, court record, or both. For qualifying post-2019 vacated convictions, the statute says the arrest and court record shall be annulled. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/new-hampshire/title-lxii/chapter-651/section-651-5/")
Wilma should ask:

Was the conviction later vacated, reversed, or set aside by the court?

Path C - Conviction Annulment Under RSA 651:5
For convictions, the person generally must:
1 Complete all terms and conditions of the sentence;
2 Have no new disqualifying conviction during the waiting period;
3 Satisfy the waiting period for the offense level;
4 Have no pending charge that would block or extend eligibility;
5 Avoid statutory bars, including violent crimes, felony obstruction-of-justice crimes, and extended-term sentences. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/new-hampshire/title-lxii/chapter-651/section-651-5/")
Wilma should explain:

For a conviction, New Hampshire usually starts counting after the sentence is fully complete, not just from the conviction date.

Path D - Marijuana Possession Annulment
New Hampshire has a special marijuana possession annulment statute, RSA 651:5-b. A person arrested or convicted before September 16, 2017 for knowingly or purposely possessing, obtaining, purchasing, or transporting 3/4 of an ounce of marijuana or less may petition the court where the arrest or conviction occurred at any time. The prosecutor has 10 days to object; if the prosecutor objects, they must prove beyond a reasonable doubt that the marijuana amount exceeded 3/4 ounce. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/new-hampshire/2019/title-lxii/chapter-651/section-651-5-b/")
Wilma intake question:

Was this for possession of marijuana, and was the amount 3/4 ounce or less before September 16, 2017?

Important update: a 2025 bill, HB 196, would have expanded cannabis annulment, but it did not become law; it was killed in the Senate. Wilma should not apply that proposed expansion as current law. ("https://www.mpp.org/states/new-hampshire/?utm_source=chatgpt.com")
Path E - DWI / DUI Annulment
DWI is a special category. New Hampshires main statute carves out RSA 265-A:21 from the ordinary waiting-period structure. 603 Legal Aid explains that convictions for driving, attempting to drive, or operating a vehicle, OHRV, or boat while under the influence generally require a 10-year waiting period after conviction before asking for annulment. ("https://www.603legalaid.org/how-to-remove-your-criminal-record")
Wilma should treat DWI as an escalation flag:

DWI has special annulment rules in New Hampshire. We need to verify the exact statute, conviction date, sentence completion, and whether any other convictions affect timing.

Path F - Federal, Military, or Out-of-State Records
New Hampshire courts can only annul New Hampshire records. 603 Legal Aid explains that New Hampshire courts cannot remove federal or military convictions, and out-of-state records require relief in the state where they occurred. ("https://www.603legalaid.org/how-to-remove-your-criminal-record")
Wilma should say:

A New Hampshire annulment petition can address New Hampshire arrests, charges, convictions, and sentences. It does not directly annul a federal, military, Tribal, or out-of-state conviction.

sourceSections.title: Conviction Waiting Periods
sourceSections.text:
The basic New Hampshire conviction waiting periods are:

Offense Type Waiting Period
Violation 1 year
Class B misdemeanor 2 years
Class A misdemeanor 3 years
Class B felony 5 years
Class A felony 10 years
Sexual assault under RSA 632-A:4 10 years
Felony indecent exposure or lewdness under RSA 645:1, II 10 years
Misdemeanor domestic violence under RSA 631:2-b 10 years
Class A misdemeanor or felony offense under RSA 318-B:26, II 2 years
DWI / DUI-type offense Special 10-year rule; verify under RSA 265-A:21
RSA 651:5 lists violation, Class B misdemeanor, Class A misdemeanor, Class B felony, Class A felony, sexual assault, felony indecent exposure/lewdness, misdemeanor domestic violence, and certain controlled-drug-act waiting periods. (

"https://law.justia.com/codes/new-hampshire/title-lxii/chapter-651/section-651-5/")
Wilma should calculate eligibility from:

the date all terms and conditions of sentence were completed,

unless a special statute calculates differently, such as certain DWI or motor-vehicle rules.

sourceSections.title: Multiple Convictions Rule
sourceSections.text:
This is a major Wilma logic point.
If a person has more than one offense, RSA 651:5 says no petition may be brought and no annulment granted:
1 If any part of the record is barred; or
2 Until the time requirements for all offenses of record have been met. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/new-hampshire/title-lxii/chapter-651/section-651-5/")
Wilma should say:

If you have multiple New Hampshire convictions, you may need to wait until every conviction on your record is eligible before the court can grant annulment.

This is one of the easiest places for Wilma to get eligibility wrong.

sourceSections.title: Offenses That Cannot Be Annulled
sourceSections.text:
RSA 651:5 bars annulment for:

Barred Category Meaning
Violent crime Defined in RSA 651:5, XIII
Felony obstruction-of-justice crimes Defined in RSA 651:5, XIV
Extended-term sentence If sentenced to an extended term under RSA 651:6
The statute defines
violent crime
to include murder, manslaughter, class A felony negligent homicide, first-degree assault, aggravated felonious sexual assault, felonious sexual assault, kidnapping, criminal restraint, class A felony arson, robbery, certain incest or child-solicitation offenses, and felonious child sexual abuse image offenses. (

"https://law.justia.com/codes/new-hampshire/title-lxii/chapter-651/section-651-5/")
Wilma should not try to make a final eligibility decision on these. Route to attorney review.

sourceSections.text:
Current public guidance from 603 Legal Aid lists:

Fee Amount
Court filing fee $125
Department of Corrections investigation fee $100
Department of Safety / State Police correction fee Up to $100
603 Legal Aid says the court filing fee can cover multiple petitions in one court if filed at the same time, and the State Police fee is generally paid after a petition is granted; there is no State Police fee for charges that did not result in conviction. (

"https://www.603legalaid.org/how-to-remove-your-criminal-record")
RSA 651:5 also references a $100 Department of Corrections investigation fee and a Department of Safety fee up to $100, with indigency exceptions and fee-waiver forms. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/new-hampshire/title-lxii/chapter-651/section-651-5/")
Wilma should ask:

Do you need a fee waiver based on income or inability to pay?

sourceSections.title: Effects of Annulment
sourceSections.text:
When annulment is granted:
The person is generally treated as if they had never been arrested, convicted, or sentenced.
The prior conviction may still be considered for sentencing if the person commits a later crime.
It may count toward habitual offender status.
The court issues a certificate.
The court notifies State Police Criminal Records, the prosecutor, and the arresting agency.
Court records are sealed from ordinary public access.
State Police removes the annulled criminal record and informs appropriate state and federal agencies.
Arresting and prosecuting agencies must identify the record as annulled.
Employment/license/civil-right questions should be phrased to ask only about records not annulled by a court. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/new-hampshire/title-lxii/chapter-651/section-651-5/")
Wilma should explain:

After an annulment, most employers and ordinary background checks should not treat the annulled case as an active public conviction, but the record may still be available for limited law-enforcement, court, sentencing, and other statutory uses.

sourceSections.title: Limits of Annulment
sourceSections.text:
Wilma must not overpromise.
603 Legal Aid states that the federal government does not recognize New Hampshire annulments, including immigration authorities and federal courts. It also warns that third-party background-check providers may not update their records regularly. ("https://www.603legalaid.org/what-happens-if-approved")
Wilma should say:

A New Hampshire annulment can be powerful for state records, but it may not solve federal immigration, federal court, federal employment, FBI, private-background-check, or out-of-state record issues.

sourceSections.title: Wilma Intake Script
sourceSections.text:
Opening

I can help you understand possible New Hampshire annulment pathways. New Hampshire usually calls this annulment, not expungement. This is legal information, not legal advice. Eligibility depends on the official court record, the offense level, the final outcome, sentence completion, and whether there are other convictions or pending charges.

Core Questions
1 Was the case in New Hampshire?
2 Which court handled it?
Circuit Court / District Division?
Superior Court?
Supreme Court?
3 What county or court location?
4 What is the case number?
5 Was this an arrest only, charge, conviction, dismissed case, not-guilty case, not-prosecuted case, or vacated conviction?
6 What was the offense name and RSA statute?
7 What was the offense level?
Violation?
Class B misdemeanor?
Class A misdemeanor?
Class B felony?
Class A felony?
8 Was this DWI / DUI / driving under the influence?
9 Was this marijuana possession of 3/4 ounce or less before September 16, 2017?
10 Was this domestic violence under RSA 631:2-b?
11 Was this sexual assault, felony indecent exposure, violent crime, obstruction of justice, or an extended-term sentence?
12 When were all sentence terms completed?
13 Have there been any new convictions since completion?
14 Are there any pending charges now?
15 Are there any other convictions on the persons record?
16 Has the person filed a prior annulment petition that was denied?
17 Does the person need annulment for employment, housing, licensing, immigration, military, school, or professional certification?
18 Does the person need a fee waiver?

sourceSections.title: Wilma Decision Tree
sourceSections.text:
If Federal, Military, Tribal, or Out-of-State
Route:

New Hampshire courts cannot annul this directly. The user needs jurisdiction-specific review.

If Not Guilty / Dismissed / Not Prosecuted
Route:

Check disposition date. If before January 1, 2019, petition may be filed at any time. If on or after January 1, 2019, check whether automatic annulment occurred 30 days after dismissal/not-guilty if no appeal.

If Vacated Conviction
Route:

Potential annulment of arrest and court record. Confirm vacatur order and date.

If Marijuana Possession
Route:

Check RSA 651:5-b: offense before September 16, 2017, possession/transport/purchase/control of 3/4 ounce or less. File in court of arrest or conviction and serve prosecutor.

If DWI / DUI
Route:

Special DWI annulment timing. Verify RSA 265-A:21 and 10-year rule. Escalate if multiple driving convictions or habitual offender issue.

If Conviction
Route:

Confirm sentence completion, offense class, waiting period, no new convictions, no pending charges, no barred offense, and all other convictions eligible.

If Multiple Convictions
Route:

Do not mark eligible until every offense of record satisfies its waiting period and no offense is barred.

If Violent Crime / Felony Obstruction / Extended-Term Sentence
Route:

Likely barred. Attorney review required.
